casual-professional.name: Casual Professional
casual-professional.description: Friendly, human, slightly informal. Like messaging a peer you met at a conference.

guidelines.vocabulary: Conversational. Contractions always. No jargon unless industry-specific and natural.
guidelines.structure: Loose. No bullet points in messages. Reads like a text, not an email.
guidelines.opener_style: Reference something specific about them. No 'I hope this message finds you well.'
guidelines.cta_style: Low-pressure question. 'Curious if...' or 'Worth a look?' — never 'I'd love to schedule a call.'
guidelines.punctuation: Periods and question marks. Minimal exclamation points. No ellipses.

good_examples.message: Hey {first_name} — saw your comment on the FreightWaves post about driver retention. We're working on the same problem from the tech side. Would be great to connect.
good_examples.why_good: Specific signal, natural language, no pitch, 198 chars

good_examples.message: Thanks for connecting, {first_name}. Noticed {company} has been growing fast — congrats. Quick question: are you still handling carrier check calls manually, or have you automated that yet?
good_examples.why_good: Brief acknowledgment, genuine observation, ends with relevant question

bad_examples.message: Hi {first_name}, I'm reaching out because I noticed your impressive profile. I work at [Company] and we help logistics companies like yours improve operational efficiency. I'd love to connect and explore potential synergies!
bad_examples.why_bad: Generic flattery, corporate jargon ('synergies'), pitches in the connection request, 247 chars of nothing

bad_examples.message: Hi {first_name}! Thanks so much for accepting my connection request! I'm really excited to be connected with you. As I mentioned, we help companies like {company} with their operations. Would you be open to a 30-minute call this week to discuss how we can help?
bad_examples.why_bad: Over-eager, sells immediately, 30-minute ask on first message, no value offered

thought-leader.name: Thought Leader
thought-leader.description: Lead with insight or a contrarian take. Position the sender as someone worth listening to.

guidelines.sentence_length: Medium. 10-20 words. Allow one longer sentence per message for the insight.
guidelines.vocabulary: Informed. Industry terms are fine. Show you understand their world deeply.
guidelines.structure: Open with an observation or insight. Follow with relevance. Close with a question that makes them think.
guidelines.opener_style: Start with a perspective, not a compliment. 'I've been noticing X trend...' or 'Most people get X wrong...'
guidelines.cta_style: Thought-provoking question. 'How are you thinking about X?' — positions conversation as peer-to-peer.
guidelines.punctuation: Standard. Dashes for emphasis. Occasional colon before a key point.

good_examples.message: Hey {first_name} — been thinking about how {industry} companies are approaching {pain_topic}. Most are solving it wrong. Would love to swap notes.
good_examples.why_good: Bold opener, implies expertise, curiosity-driven, 168 chars

good_examples.message: Here's what I keep seeing: companies throw headcount at {pain_topic} instead of rethinking the process. The ones winning are the ones who automate the repetitive parts and redeploy people to higher-value work. Curious how {company} is approaching this.
good_examples.why_good: Shares a genuine insight, doesn't pitch product, asks for their perspective

bad_examples.message: Hi {first_name}, as a thought leader in the {industry} space, I wanted to connect with fellow innovators disrupting the status quo. Let's connect!
bad_examples.why_bad: Self-proclaimed thought leader, buzzword soup, no actual insight

bad_examples.message: I've been published in Forbes, spoke at 3 conferences this year, and our company was just named a top innovator. I think you'd benefit from learning about our approach.
bad_examples.why_bad: All about the sender, condescending, credential-dumping

provocative.name: Provocative
provocative.description: Challenge assumptions, pattern-interrupt. Higher risk, higher reward. Best for saturated markets where everyone sounds the same.

guidelines.sentence_length: Short and punchy. 5-12 words. Staccato rhythm.
guidelines.vocabulary: Direct. Blunt. No hedging words ('maybe', 'perhaps', 'I think'). State things plainly.
guidelines.structure: Open with a bold claim or question. Back it up briefly. End with a challenge or dare.
guidelines.opener_style: Start with something unexpected. A question, a counterintuitive stat, or a bold statement.
guidelines.cta_style: Challenge format. 'Want me to prove it?' or 'Bet you $5 I'm right.' — playful confidence.
guidelines.punctuation: Short sentences. Periods for impact. Questions to provoke. Never exclamation points.

good_examples.message: {first_name} — your team is probably losing 20+ hours/week on something that shouldn't exist anymore. Happy to be wrong. Let's connect.
good_examples.why_good: Bold claim, acknowledges they might disagree, invites debate, 156 chars

good_examples.message: Quick math: if {company} has 5 people doing {pain_topic} manually, that's 100+ hours/month. At $40/hr loaded cost, that's $48K/year on a task a machine handles in seconds. Where am I wrong?
good_examples.why_good: Specific, quantified, challenges them to engage, shows you did homework

bad_examples.message: Stop what you're doing. Your current approach to {pain_topic} is costing you millions. I can fix it in 30 days. Connect with me now.
bad_examples.why_bad: Aggressive without substance, unsubstantiated claims, demands action

bad_examples.message: I'm going to be blunt: your competitors are already using AI and you're falling behind. Every day you wait is a day they get further ahead. Can we talk?

enterprise-formal.name: Enterprise Formal
enterprise-formal.description: Polished, structured, respectful of hierarchy. For regulated industries, large enterprises, or C-suite targets.

guidelines.sentence_length: Medium to long. 12-25 words. Complete, well-structured sentences.
guidelines.vocabulary: Professional. Industry-appropriate terminology. Avoid slang. Formal but not stiff.
guidelines.structure: Clear intro, context, value proposition, respectful ask. Each message has a logical flow.
guidelines.opener_style: Reference a specific, verifiable business context. Company news, earnings call, industry report.
guidelines.cta_style: Respectful and specific. 'Would a brief conversation be worthwhile?' — always offer an easy out.
guidelines.punctuation: Standard formal punctuation. No dashes for emphasis. Occasional em-dash in longer sentences.

good_examples.message: {first_name}, I noticed {company}'s recent expansion into {market}. We've supported similar transitions for companies in {industry}. I'd welcome the chance to connect.
good_examples.why_good: Specific business context, credibility signal, respectful tone, 191 chars

good_examples.message: Thank you for connecting, {first_name}. Given {company}'s growth trajectory, I thought you might find this relevant: we recently helped a {industry} company reduce their {pain_topic} processing time by 60% while maintaining full compliance. Would it be worthwhile to share the specifics?
good_examples.why_good: Grateful without gushing, specific result, compliance mention for regulated industries, asks permission

bad_examples.message: Dear {first_name}, I am writing to introduce myself and my organization. We are a leading provider of solutions in the {industry} space and I believe there may be mutual benefit in establishing a professional connection.
bad_examples.why_bad: Reads like a cold email from 2005, no specifics, 'mutual benefit' is meaningless

bad_examples.message: Per my previous message, I wanted to follow up regarding the opportunity I mentioned. Please find attached our company brochure and case studies. I would appreciate the opportunity to present our solutions at your earliest convenience.
bad_examples.why_bad: Can't attach on LinkedIn, 'per my previous message' is passive-aggressive, no value
